title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
Macro Circuit Simulation


Toggle the pump. Watch the Pressure (Voltage) and Flow (Current) gauges.

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
The Closed Loop





The pump creates pressure — the force that pushes the water




The actual amount of water moving is the flow




The narrow part of the pipe is resistance — it fights the flow









These three are always connected.


You cannot change one without affecting the others.










↑ Pressure → ↑ Flow
↑ Resistance → ↓ Flow

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
Series vs. Parallel Pipes




Series: Pipes connected in a single line.

All water must pass through every pipe.
Same Flow throughout the line.
Pressure is shared between them.





Parallel: Pipes connected in a branch.

Water chooses a path.
Flow splits between branches.
Same Pressure at the start of each path.

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
Exercise 1 — Q1: Narrow Pipe
(recall) If I make the pipe narrower at one point, what happens to the water flow?

Answer: Flow decreases — the narrowing increases resistance, so less water passes through for the same pump pressure.

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
Exercise 1 — Q2: Second Pump
(recall) If I add a second pump, what happens?

Answer: It depends on how you connect them:

In Series (one after the other): Pressure increases → flow increases. (Like batteries in a flashlight).
In Parallel (side-by-side): Pressure stays the same, but you have more total flow capacity. (The pumps share the work).

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
Exercise 1 — Q3: Cut Pipe
(recall) If I cut the pipe somewhere, what happens?

Answer: Flow stops completely — the loop is broken and water has nowhere to go.

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
Exercise 1 — Q4: Stronger Pump
(recall) If the pump gets stronger, what happens?

Answer: More pressure → more flow. With the same resistance, higher pressure drives more water through the pipe.

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
Exercise 2 — Q1: Compensate for Narrowing
(apply) You narrow the pipe a lot. What must the pump do to keep the same flow rate?

Answer: The pump must increase its pressure proportionally — to maintain the same flow against higher resistance, you need more force (higher voltage in the electron world).

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
Exercise 2 — Q2: All Valves Open
(apply) Someone opens ALL the valves at once. What happens to the flow at each valve?

Answer: The total flow increases but splits between all open paths. Each valve gets less flow than it would alone — parallel paths share the total.

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
section: Exercise 2 — Q3: Pressure Before a Narrowing
text:
Exercise 2 — Q3: Pressure Before a Narrowing
(design) What happens to the pressure just before a sudden narrowing in the pipe?

Answer: Pressure backs up just before the narrowing — molecules pile up waiting to squeeze through. Analogous to the high voltage level that exists on the upstream side of a resistor before it drops across it.

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
Micro Pump Simulation


Zoom into the pump impeller. Watch individual particles collide and push each other.

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
section: What the Molecules Are Telling You
text:
What the Molecules Are Telling You




Water is not a smooth fluid — it is discrete molecules crowding and pushing




Pressure is the collective “pushing force” created when molecules pile up and collide tightly together




Flow is the actual amount of those molecules managing to pass by a specific point every second




Resistance is energy lost to collisions with the pipe wall — the narrower the pipe, the more collisions per metre









Why this matters for electrons


Current is not a fluid either. It is a wave of pushes through particles already packed into the wire. The pump doesn’t create them — it just gives them a direction.

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
Exercise 3 — Q1: Blocked Pipe
(apply) If you completely block the pipe, the pump is still running. What happens to the pressure just before the block? What happens to the flow everywhere?

Answer: Pressure reaches maximum just before the block (all pump force with nowhere to go). Flow everywhere = 0 — no complete loop means no movement anywhere in the circuit.

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
Exercise 3 — Q2: Friction and Heat
(design) What if the molecules were going so fast through a narrow section that the friction made them heat up? What do you think would happen to the pipe material?

Answer: The pipe wall at the narrow section heats up and could melt or deform — exactly what happens to a wire or LED with too much current. This is how a fuse works: the narrow link melts to break the circuit safely.

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
Water World ↔︎ Electron World



Water World

Electronics




Water molecules
→
Electrons


Pump
→
Battery / power supply


Pressure
→
Voltage (V)


Flow rate
→
Current (A)


Narrow pipe
→
Resistance (Ω)









Same physics. Different scale. Different particles.


The battery doesn’t create electrons — they’re already there. It just creates pressure.









Wait — which direction does current flow?


Electrons (negative charges) actually drift from − to + inside the wire. But engineers defined “current direction” centuries before electrons were discovered — they picked + to −. Both descriptions are correct. In this course we always follow the + to − convention.

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
See the Translation Live


Use the Voltage and Resistance sliders. Watch Current change — exactly as in the water world.

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
Electromagnetic Repulsion Simulation


Watch electrons repel each other before ever touching. Connect the battery — see the drift begin.

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
Conductors vs Insulators


Conductors (e.g. copper)

Outer electrons are loosely bound
They roam freely through the material
Apply voltage → they drift together → current flows







Why metals conduct


Metallic bonds leave outer electrons free to drift. That freedom is exactly what makes a wire a wire.




Insulators (e.g. rubber, glass)

Electrons are locked tightly to their atoms
No free electrons → no drift → no current







High enough voltage breaks insulators


Rip the electrons free and even air conducts. That is lightning.

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
section: Exercise 5 — Q1: Where is the Friction?
text:
Exercise 5 — Q1: Where is the Friction?
(apply) If electrons push each other without touching, why does a wire feel like it has resistance? Where is the “friction” really coming from?

Answer: Electrons collide with the metal’s lattice of positive ions (atoms). Each collision transfers kinetic energy to the lattice as heat — that is resistance. The friction is not between electrons; it is between electrons and the stationary atomic structure.

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
Exercise 5 — Q2: Breaking an Insulator
(design) An insulator has its electrons locked tight. What would happen if you applied an extremely high voltage — high enough to rip those electrons free? (Think: lightning through air.)

Answer: The insulator breaks down — electrons are violently stripped from their atoms and a sudden rush of current flows. Air does exactly this during lightning: enormous voltage rips electrons free and the air briefly becomes a conductor.

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
text:
Ohm’s Law

V = I × R

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
section: Three Forms of the Same Truth
text:
Three Forms of the Same Truth
You only need one formula — rearrange for what you don’t know:



Find
Formula
Units




Voltage
V = I × R
Volts (V)


Current
I = V / R
Amperes (A)


Resistance
R = V / I
Ohms (Ω)




If pressure goes up and resistance stays the same, flow increases. Same physics. Same formula.

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
text:
Ohm’s Law Sandbox


Calculate a target Current first. Then adjust the Voltage and Resistance sliders — does the gauge match your math?

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
text:
Exercise 6 — Q1: Calculate Current
(recall) A battery provides 5 V. A resistor of 100 Ω is connected. How much current flows?
Show your calculation.

Answer: I = V / R = 5 / 100 = 0.05 A (50 mA)

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
text:
Exercise 6 — Q2: Calculate Resistance
(apply) A circuit has 12 V and the current is 0.1 A. What is the resistance?
Show your calculation.

Answer: R = V / I = 12 / 0.1 = 120 Ω

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
section: Exercise 6 — Q3: Halve or Double Resistance
text:
Exercise 6 — Q3: Halve or Double Resistance
(design) Challenge: If you halve the resistance in a circuit with fixed voltage, what happens to the current? If you double the resistance — what happens?

Answer: Since I = V / R and V is fixed:

Halve R → current doubles
Double R → current halves

Current and resistance are inversely proportional.

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
Micro Light Bulb Simulation


Watch particles squeeze through the jagged filament. As they collide they turn orange/red. At high pressure, the whole area glows.

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
The Light Bulb


Inside a bulb: a filament — a very thin, very resistive wire (tungsten).

Electrons squeeze through the narrow filament
Constant collisions with tungsten atoms → friction → heat
Filament reaches ~2,500°C → glows → light


Narrow pipe → friction → heat → glow
Same physics as the water world.






The weakest, most resistive part always heats the most.


This is why LEDs need resistors. Without one: low resistance + fixed voltage = enormous current → burnout in milliseconds.

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
The LED (Light Emitting Diode)




An LED is a One-Way Check Valve.
It only allows current to flow in one direction.
If connected backward, the “valve” shuts tight → Zero Flow.








Identification



Long Leg = Positive (+)
Short Leg (and flat side of plastic) = Negative (−)
Current must flow from + to −.

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
Exercise 7 — Q1: Calculate Current (No Resistor)
A student wires an LED directly to 3.3 V with no resistor. The LED has an approximate resistance of ~10 Ω when lit (LEDs are non-ohmic — this is a simplified model for calculation only).
(recall) Using I = V / R, calculate how much current flows.

Answer: I = 3.3 / 10 = 0.33 A = 330 mA
(That is 11× the maximum rated current!)

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
section: Exercise 7 — Q2: What Happens to the LED?
text:
Exercise 7 — Q2: What Happens to the LED?
Current without a resistor = 330 mA. The LED is rated for a maximum of 30 mA.
(recall) What happens?

Answer: The LED burns out instantly — 330 mA is 11× the maximum. The semiconductor junction overheats in milliseconds and is permanently destroyed.

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
Exercise 7 — Q3: Filament vs LED
(apply) Why does a tungsten filament survive high temperatures but an LED doesn’t?

Answer: Tungsten has the highest melting point of any metal (~3 422 °C) and is designed to glow. An LED is a semiconductor junction — it converts current to light very efficiently but is destroyed by excess heat. It was never built to survive white-hot temperatures.

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
section: Exercise 7 — Q4: Design a Safe Circuit
text:
Exercise 7 — Q4: Design a Safe Circuit
(design) Design the safe version: what resistor limits current to 20 mA? (V = 3.3 V, LED voltage drop = 2 V)

Answer:
Voltage across resistor = 3.3 − 2.0 = 1.3 V
R = V / I = 1.3 / 0.02 = 65 Ω
Nearest standard value: 68 Ω (or 100 Ω for extra safety — slightly less current, LED lives longer).

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
Micro Motor Simulation


Watch electrons (particles) interact with the magnetic armature. North (Red) and South (Blue) poles are pushed and pulled by the moving charge.

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
The Motor



Moving electrons create a magnetic field
Inside the motor: permanent magnets on the outside, electromagnets (coils) on the inside
Current through coils → magnetic field → interacts with permanent magnets
Opposite poles attract → like poles repel → rotation
Current flips direction at the right moment → poles flip → constant push-pull → continuous spin


Flow → Magnetic Force → Motion
Electrons moving
     ↓
Magnetic field created
     ↓
EM force on magnets
     ↓
Rotation

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
The Switch


A switch is a gap in the circuit that can be opened or closed.

Closed (switch on): complete path → current flows
Open (switch off): broken loop → current = zero







Voltage without current


When a switch is open, voltage still exists across the gap. That is why you can measure it with a multimeter. Current requires a complete path — voltage does not.




Pump running   → Voltage exists
Loop complete  → Current flows
Loop broken    → Current = 0

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
Exercise 8 — Q1: Resistive Heating
(apply) What is the electron-world equivalent?








Situation
Water world
Electron world




Water flows fast through thin pipe, pipe gets warm
Friction between molecules and pipe wall
?




Answer: Electrons collide with atoms in the resistive wire — kinetic energy converts to heat. This is resistive (Joule) heating: the basis of how a resistor or filament gets hot.

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
Exercise 8 — Q2: Open Valve
(apply) What is the water-world equivalent?



Situation
Water world
Electron world




Flow is completely stopped
?
Switch in open position




Answer: A closed gate valve — the valve is physically shut, breaking the loop so water (current) cannot flow.

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
Exercise 8 — Q3: Paddle Wheel
(apply) What is the electron-world equivalent?








Situation
Water world
Electron world




Paddle wheel converts flow to rotation
Water pressure turns the paddle
?




Answer: Current through a coil creates a magnetic field → force on permanent magnets → rotation. This is an electric motor.

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
Exercise 8 — Q4: Melting Section
(apply) What is the water-world equivalent?



Situation
Water world
Electron world




A section so narrow it melts
?
LED with no resistor




Answer: A pipe section so narrow that friction heats the material until it melts — equivalent to a fuse blowing, or an LED junction destroyed by overcurrent.

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
text:
Variable Resistance: The Potentiometer

A valve in cross-section: a disc with a hole — rotate it to partially block flow
In electronics: a potentiometer has a resistive strip + sliding wiper
Move the wiper → change the resistance → change the current

Where you see this: Volume knobs · Brightness dimmers · Steering sensitivity controls



Position
Resistance
Current




Fully open
0 Ω
Maximum


Midpoint
~250 Ω
Medium


Fully closed
Max (e.g. 10 kΩ)
Zero

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
The Valve Simulation


Manually rotate the valve. Watch the current change in real time.

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
text:
The Automatic Valve: The Transistor


Manual Valve (Potentiometer)

You turn it by hand
Analog: smooth range of values
Input: your hand


Transistor

Electricity turns it — automatically
A tiny Base current controls a huge Collector current
1 mA Base → 200 mA Collector (gain = 200×)
Input: another electron signal



“A tiny trickle opens the main floodgate.” This is amplification — and it is also logic.

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
text:
Transistors Make Logic

Transistor fully ON → effectively zero resistance → current flows → “1”
Transistor fully OFF → enormous resistance → no current → “0”

A billion of these = a CPU.

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
text:
Exercise 9 — Q1: Potentiometer at 0 Ω
You have: 5 V supply · LED (20 mA, 2 V drop) · Potentiometer (0–500 Ω) · Fixed 150 Ω resistor
(recall) Potentiometer at 0 Ω: what current flows through the LED? (Use V = 3 V; derived from 5 V supply − 2 V LED drop)

Answer: I = V / R = 3 / (0 + 150) = 0.02 A = 20 mA — LED at rated maximum brightness.

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
text:
Exercise 9 — Q2: Potentiometer at 500 Ω
You have: 5 V supply · LED (20 mA, 2 V drop) · Potentiometer (0–500 Ω) · Fixed 150 Ω resistor
(apply) Potentiometer at 500 Ω: what current flows?

Answer: I = 3 / (500 + 150) = 3 / 650 ≈ 4.6 mA — LED is very dim, just below usable threshold.

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
section: Exercise 9 — Q3: Rotating from 0 to 500 Ω
text:
Exercise 9 — Q3: Rotating from 0 to 500 Ω
You have: 5 V supply · LED (20 mA, 2 V drop) · Potentiometer (0–500 Ω) · Fixed 150 Ω resistor
(apply) What does the LED do as you slowly rotate from 0 to 500 Ω?

Answer: The LED dims gradually — from 20 mA (full brightness) down to ~4.6 mA (barely visible). The potentiometer acts as a variable brightness (dimmer) control.

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
text:
Exercise 9 — Q4: Find the Cut-off
You have: 5 V supply · LED (20 mA, 2 V drop) · Potentiometer (0–500 Ω) · Fixed 150 Ω resistor
(design) At what potentiometer value does current drop below the LED’s minimum threshold (5 mA)?

Answer: I = 3 / (R + 150) < 0.005 → R + 150 > 600 → R > 450 Ω
At 450 Ω the LED falls below 5 mA and effectively goes out.

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
text:
Exercise 10 — Q1: Transistor Gain
(recall) A transistor has a gain of 200. You apply 0.5 mA to the Base. How much current flows through the Collector?

Answer: I_C = Gain × I_B = 200 × 0.5 mA = 100 mA
A tiny 0.5 mA signal controls a 100 mA load — that is amplification.

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
text:
Exercise 10 — Q2: Controlling a Motor
(apply) You want to control a 500 mA motor using an Arduino GPIO pin (max 40 mA). Is this possible? What is the transistor’s role?

Answer: Yes — but not directly. The GPIO cannot source 500 mA safely. A transistor with gain ≥ 13 driven by the GPIO switches the 500 mA motor current from a separate supply. The GPIO controls the gate; the transistor carries the load.

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
section: Exercise 10 — Q3: ON vs OFF = 1 vs 0
text:
Exercise 10 — Q3: ON vs OFF = 1 vs 0
(apply) A transistor is either fully ON (0.1 Ω) or fully OFF (1 000 000 Ω). Which state is “1” and which is “0”?

Answer:

Fully ON (0.1 Ω) → near-zero resistance → current flows → “1”
Fully OFF (1 MΩ) → near-infinite resistance → no current → “0”

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
text:
Two Worlds: Analog vs. Digital


Analog — Infinite Steps


Dimmer switch: 0% to 100% brightness
Thermometer: any value in a continuous range
LDR sensor: variable (1 kΩ to 100 kΩ)



Digital — Two States Only


A light switch: ON or OFF
A transistor: fully ON or fully OFF
A GPIO pin: HIGH (3.3 V) or LOW (0 V)









Analog = continuous
Infinite possible values between any two points.









Digital = discrete
Only two possible states. No in-between.







The bridge: A transistor collapses an analog input voltage into a digital decision — above a threshold → 1, below it → 0. A billion of these decisions per second = a CPU.

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
The Ladder of Abstraction
From transistors to C++ — five rungs:

Voltage — above ~3V = HIGH (1), below ~1V = LOW (0)
Binary — 1 bit = on/off · 8 bits = 1 byte = 256 values
Machine Code — patterns of 0s and 1s the processor reads directly
Assembly — human-readable commands, assembled to machine code
C++ — high-level logic, compiled into machine code for the Arduino







C++ is not what the computer runs directly


Your code is compiled — turned into a binary file — containing the 0s and 1s that transistors actually process.

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
Meet the Arduino Pins


Every pin is a software-defined switch.
OUTPUT — The Driver ⚡

Forces 5V (HIGH) or 0V (LOW).
Drives LEDs, motors, buzzers.

INPUT — The Listener 👂

Measures voltage signals.
Reads buttons, sensors.







GPIO = General Purpose Input / Output

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
Programming the Pins






Mode is Mandatory


A pin defaults to Input for safety. If you forget pinMode(pin, OUTPUT), your LED will never light because the pin is “listening,” not “pushing.”






Code Pattern
Physical Effect




pinMode(13, OUTPUT)
Pin 13 configured to drive voltage


pinMode(12, INPUT)
Pin 12 configured to listen for voltage


digitalWrite(13, HIGH)
Switch Pin 13 ON (5 V)


digitalWrite(13, LOW)
Switch Pin 13 OFF (0 V)


analogRead(A0)
Read fractional voltage (0–1023)

text:
Exercise 11 — Q4: OUTPUT vs INPUT
(apply) What is the difference between OUTPUT and INPUT? What would happen if you forgot pinMode(pin, OUTPUT)?

Answer:

OUTPUT — the Arduino drives the pin to 5 V or 0 V
INPUT — the Arduino reads the voltage on the pin; it drives nothing

Forgetting pinMode → pin defaults to input mode → outputs no voltage → LED stays off.

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
Meet the Breadboard
A breadboard lets you build circuits without soldering.


The Internal Logic

Terminal rows (A–J) — Holes in a row are connected.
Power rails (+ / −) — Run the full length.
Centre divider — Splits the board in two.

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
Powering the Board: The Rails


To give your circuit life, you need Pressure (V) and a Drain (GND).

Red Rail (+): Connect to 5V or 3.3V.
Blue Rail (−): Connect to GND (The Ground).


Wiring the Rails (Do This First!)

One wire from Arduino 5V pin → red rail.
One wire from Arduino GND pin → blue rail.
Now every row on the board can draw power or connect to ground.








Relative Ground


All voltages are measured relative to GND (0V). Think of it as the water level in the drain — everything flows toward it.











Pro tip


Wire the Rails first! Then you can pull power for your components from anywhere on the long side strips.

Build #2 — The Voltage Divider
How does a light sensor become a number the Arduino can read?

               (A0)

Fixed 10 kΩ stays constant
Midpoint voltage rises as LDR gets smaller









Water analogy


Two resistors in series = two narrow pipes. The pressure tap between them reads a fraction of total — depending on which section is narrower.

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
section: The ADC — Bridging Analog and Digital
text:
The ADC — Bridging Analog and Digital
The Arduino thinks in digital (0s and 1s). The LDR gives us analog (a continuous voltage). The ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter) is the translator.




The ADC samples the midpoint voltage thousands of times per second
It maps that voltage to a 10-bit number: 0 → 1023






Voltage
analogRead() value




0 V (max bright)
0


2.5 V (half)
~512


5.0 V (max dark)
1023












Why 1023?


10 bits can hold 2¹⁰ = 1,024 values (0 to 1023). Arduino Uno uses 10-bit resolution for its analog pins.











In code


int val = analogRead(A0); — take one sample from analog pin 0 and return the number.

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic

text:
Exercise 13 — Q1: ADC Voltage
(recall) What voltage does analogRead() = 512 represent? (Hint: it is half the full 5.0 V range)

Answer: 512 is half of 1023 ≈ half-scale → 2.5 V (half of 5.0 V).

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
Exercise 13 — Q2: Change the Threshold
(apply) DARK_THRESHOLD is changed to 800. Does the LED now switch on in dimmer or brighter conditions? Why?

Answer: Dimmer — the sensor must return a higher value (800) before the LED switches on. Higher ADC values mean less light, so the room must be darker than before to trigger the LED.

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
section: Exercise 13 — Q3: Add a Second LED
text:
Exercise 13 — Q3: Add a Second LED
(apply) Add a second green LED on Pin 11 that turns ON when the room is bright. Write the extra two lines of code.

Answer:
pinMode(11, OUTPUT);        // add in setup()

// inside loop(), add:
digitalWrite(11, ldrReading > DARK_THRESHOLD ? LOW : HIGH);
When bright, ldrReading > DARK_THRESHOLD is false → the green LED turns HIGH.

The Master Translation Table








Concept
Water Analogy
Electronics Reality




Voltage (V)

Force pushing electrons


Current (I)

P = F × R

HIGH / LOW voltage states

Instructions to the processor
High-level language the compiler translates

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
text:
Homework
Part A — Calculations

A 9V battery + 270Ω resistor. What current flows?
A circuit draws 50mA from 5V. What is the total resistance?
Dim an LED to 10mA using a 5V Digital pin (LED drops 2V). What resistor do you need?
A motor needs 500mA. Can an Arduino Digital pin (max 20–40mA) drive it directly? Use AI to explain the transistor solution.

Part B — Observation
Find 3 places in your home where a reactive system turns on/off based on a sensor. For each: What is the sensor? What is the action? Write 3-line C++ pseudo-code for how it might work.

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
text:
Common Code Mistakes
C++ is very picky. Watch out for these:

The Missing Semicolon (;) — Every instruction must end with one. It’s like a period at the end of a sentence.
Case Sensitivity — digitalWrite is correct. digitalwrite or DigitalWrite will cause an error.
The Curly Braces ({ }) — They group code together. Every { needs a matching }.
Incorrect Pin Numbers — Make sure the number in your code matches the physical hole on the Arduino.







Error messages are your friends


If your code won’t upload, read the red text at the bottom. It usually tells you exactly which line has the “expected ‘;’” or “was not declared in this scope.”

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
DANGER: The Short Circuit


A Short Circuit happens when you connect 5V directly to GND with a bare wire.

Resistance ≈ 0 \(\Omega\) (no obstacle)
Flow (Current) ≈ \(\infty\) (dangerous surge!)


This generates intense heat and will fry your Arduino’s chip or your laptop’s USB port instantly.

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
text:
Component: Identifying the LED
LEDs are polar — they only work in one direction.
How to tell which side is which:

The Legs: The Longer leg is the Anode (+). Connect this to the positive voltage (5V or a pin).
The Flat Side: Look closely at the plastic bulb. The side with a flat edge is the Cathode (-). This goes to GND.







“A” for Anode, “A” for Addition (+)


If you connect it backwards, it won’t break, but it won’t light up!

section: The Serial Monitor: Seeing the Invisible
text:
The Serial Monitor: Seeing the Invisible
How do you know what the Light Sensor is actually “seeing”? You ask the Arduino to print the numbers to your screen.

Start Communication: Add Serial.begin(9600); in setup().
Print the Value: Use Serial.println(ldrReading); in loop().







The Glass Window


In the Arduino IDE or Wokwi, look for the Serial Monitor (usually a magnifying glass icon). It opens a window where you can watch the values live!

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
Floating Pins & Noise
Imagine a sensor wire that isn’t connected to anything. It doesn’t read 0. It acts like an antenna, picking up random electrical noise from the air.

Floating state: The value “bits” or “bounces” between 0 and 1023 randomly.
Solution: Use a Pull-up or Pull-down resistor to “tie” the pin to a known voltage (GND or 5V) when no signal is present.







Note


This is why our button lab or sensor lab needs that 10kΩ resistor — it keeps the pin “stable.”

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
text:
A Quick Question…


How many numbers are there between 0 and 1?


Can’t be counted… Infinity!
(0.1, 0.11, 0.111, 0.5000001…)

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
text:
The “X-Ray” View


If you peeled off the plastic, you’d see metal clips inside.


Each clip grabs onto the legs of your components.
The 5 holes in a row (e.g. A10, B10, C10, D10, E10) are all connected by one piece of metal.

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
text:
Common Mistake: The “Same-Row Short”


If you plug both legs of a resistor into the same row, the current skips the resistor!


The metal clip is a “highway” with zero resistance.
The resistor is a “rocky path”.
Electrons always take the highway.









The fix


Components must bridge two different rows to do their job.





 (Imagine a resistor with both legs in Row 10)

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
text:
How to Read the Grid


The breadboard has “addresses” just like a map:

Letters (A-J): Identify the columns.
Numbers (1-30): Identify the rows.








Example


If I say: “Plug a leg into A10”, look for row 10 and column A.

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
section: The “X-Ray” View: Why Columns Stick Together
text:
The “X-Ray” View: Why Columns Stick Together


If you peeled off the plastic, you’d see metal clips inside.


Each clip grabs onto the legs of your components.
The 5 holes in a row (e.g. A10, B10, C10, D10, E10) are all connected by one piece of metal.

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
section: Bridging the Gap: Correct vs. Incorrect
text:
Bridging the Gap: Correct vs. Incorrect


A component must bridge two different metal clips to work.


WRONG: Both legs in the SAME row. The metal clip is a “shortcut” that skips your component entirely.




RIGHT: Each leg in a DIFFERENT row. Current is forced to travel through your component.

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
text:
The Resistor “Leap”


To make a circuit, components must “hop” between different metal clips.

Method 1: The Side Hop
One leg in Row 5, other leg in Row 10.


Method 2: The Divider Leap
One leg on the Left (E10), other leg on the Right (F10).

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
text:
Spot the Error!
Which connection attempt is Wrong and why?


A
 (LED legs in Row 5 and Row 6)

B
 (Resistor legs both in Row 10)

C
 (Button straddling the centre divider)


Answer: B is wrong. Since both legs are in the same metal clip (Row 10), the electricity takes the shortcut through the clip and ignores the resistor!

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
text:
Bridging the Gap: Interactive Demo
To understand how to connect components, watch this Live Simulation.


What to look for:

Left (Green LED): The resistor bridges two different rows. Current is forced to go through it. (Works!)
Right (Red LED): The resistor is in the SAME row. Electricity takes the shortcut through the metal clip and ignores the resistor. (Fails!)








The Discovery


If you don’t “bridge” a gap, your component is invisible to the electricity.

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
text:
Build #1 — The “Pressure Drop” Analogy


Thinking with water:

Pump (5V): The battery/Arduino pushing water.
Turbine (LED): Needs 2.0 V of “pressure” to spin.
Restriction (Resistor): Squeezes the pipe to “waste” the leftover pressure.








The Shared Burden


The total pressure (5V) must be used up. If the LED only takes 2V, the resistor must handle the remaining 3.0 V.





 (Pump → Narrow Pipe → Turbine → Drain)

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
text:
Build #2 — The “Two Faucets” Analogy


How do we “see” light with an Arduino?

Fixed Pipe (10kΩ): Always stays the same width.
Variable Pipe (LDR): Changes width based on light.
Pressure Gauge (A0): Measures the “pressure” trapped in the middle.








Why it works


If the LDR pipe (GND) is wide open (bright light), the pressure at the gauge falls to 0V. If it clogs up (darkness), the pressure builds up towards 5V.





 (Gauge needle moves as LDR width changes!)

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
Build #2 — Reading the Light

  5V ───── 10 kΩ (Top Pipe) ────●──── LDR (Bottom Drain) ───── GND
                                ↑
                          Pressure Gauge
                               (A0)





Light Level
Drain State
Resistance
Gauge Pressure (V)




Bright
Wide Open
LOW
Drops towards GND (0V)


Dark
Clogged
HIGH
Builds towards 5V











Why use two resistors?


Arduino pins cannot directly read resistance. They can only measure Voltage (relative pressure). We MUST use a fixed resistor to create a “bottleneck”. This converts the sensor’s changing resistance into a changing pressure that the Arduino can read!

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
text:
Build #2 — The “Smart Drain” Analogy


How do we “read” light as a number? Imagine water flowing from the 5V pump down to the GND drain:

Top Pipe (10kΩ Fixed): Always restricts the flow by the same amount.
Bottom Pipe (LDR Variable): A “smart drain” that changes width based on light.

Bright light: The drain opens naturally wide.
Darkness: The drain gets clogged and narrow.









The Pressure Balance


The Arduino pin (A0) acts as a Pressure Gauge between the pipes.

When bright (Wide Drain): Water escapes easily down the drain. Pressure at the gauge drops to 0V.
When dark (Clogged Drain): Water gets “backed up.” Pressure at the gauge builds up to 5V.












What about the “Closed Loop” rule?


The closed loop is the main path: 5V → Top Pipe → Bottom Drain → GND. The A0 pin doesn’t break this because no water flows into A0. A good pressure gauge is sealed—it simply “feels” the pressure from the outside without stealing any water!





 (Gauge needle moves as LDR width changes!)

Define the Goal (What should happen?)

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
The Language of Design: Schematics
If we want to build a house, we look at a blueprint. In electronics, that’s a Schematic. It shows the logic of the connections.


Why symbols?

Universal: Read across borders.
Clear: No messy wire photos.

Watch the yellow dots (current). Increase the voltage or decrease the resistance—what happens to the speed?

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
text:
Design Exercise: Safe LED
(design) Target: Limit current to 20 mA using a 3.3 V supply. (Assume LED voltage drop = 2 V)

Step 1: Find the Resistor Voltage
\(V_{resistor} = V_{supply} - V_{led} = 3.3V - 2.0V = 1.3V\)
Step 2: Calculate Resistance
\(R = V / I = 1.3V / 0.02A = 65 \Omega\)
Step 3: Select Standard Value
Nearest standard: **68 . Always round UP for safety. 100 is also common for “dimmer but safer” LEDs.*

Breadboard (The Reality)

Engineering isn’t just “plugging things in”—it’s a deliberate, repeatable cycle of prediction and observation:

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
text:
Reading a Schematic: The Rules






The Standard Flow



Inputs on the Left, Outputs on the Right.
Positive Voltage (+V) at the TOP.
Ground (GND) at the BOTTOM. Current flows like water: Downhill from Power to Ground.

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
text:
Intuition #1: The “Pressure Drop”


Thinking with water:

Pump (5V): The power source pushing water.
Turbine (LED): Needs exactly 2.0 V to spin correctly.
Restriction (Resistor): Squeezes the pipe to “waste” the leftover pressure.








The Complete Cycle


The water doesn’t just “hit” the drain—it flows through it and returns to the pump to be pushed again. Without the return path (GND), there is no flow.





 (Pump → Resistor → Turbine → Return Path)

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
text:
Intuition #2: The “Smart Drain”


How do we “read” light as a number?

Top Pipe (10kΩ Fixed): Restricts flow by a set amount.
Bottom Pipe (LDR Variable): Opens or closes based on light.








The Pressure Balance


The Arduino pin (A0) acts as a Pressure Gauge between the pipes.

Bright (Wide Drain): Water escapes easily through the bottom pipe. Gauge drops to 0V.
Dark (Clogged Drain): Water gets backed up. Gauge builds to 5V.












It’s Still a Loop!


Even though A0 just “listens,” the main water path is a continuous loop from the pump, through both pipes, and back to the start.





 (Closed loop through both restrictions)

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
text:
Design Heuristics (Pro Tips)
When you’re designing a quick prototype, start with these “Goldilocks” values:

**220 - 330 *

Safe & Bright.

How to Read Color Codes


Each band is a digit: the first two give the number, the third is the multiplier.

The “Finger Test”


If you build a circuit and a component feels hot to the touch—UNPLUG IT. Something is wired incorrectly or a resistor is too small.

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
text:
KVL — Kirchhoff’s Voltage Law


The Rule: Every volt added by the battery must be dropped (used up) somewhere in the loop. Nothing leaks out.
\[\text{Voltage}_{\text{Supply}} = V_1 + V_2 + V_3 \dots\]







Water analogy


The pump’s pressure gets squeezed out across every restriction. You can’t exit the loop with leftover pressure — it all gets used up before returning to the pump.





Why it matters: It’s how we calculate the resistor value in any LED circuit — the resistor must absorb whatever voltage the LED doesn’t use.




Adjust R₁ and R₂ — watch the voltage split, but the sum always stays 5 V.

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
text:
KCL — Kirchhoff’s Current Law


The Rule: Whatever current flows into a junction must flow out of it. Charge cannot pile up.
\[\text{Current}_{\text{In}} = I_1 + I_2 + I_3 \dots\]







Water analogy


At a pipe T-junction, flow splits between branches — but every drop that entered must come out the other side. Nothing disappears.





Why it matters: This is the basis of the voltage divider — two parallel paths sharing the same source voltage, each drawing its own current.




Adjust R₁ and R₂ — the dot density in each branch shows its current.

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
section: Design Exercise: Safe LED — KVL in Action
text:
Design Exercise: Safe LED — KVL in Action
(design) Target: Limit current to 20 mA using a 3.3 V supply. (Assume LED voltage drop = 2 V)



Step 1: Apply KVL — Find the Resistor Voltage
\(V_{resistor} = V_{supply} - V_{led} = 3.3V - 2.0V = 1.3V\) KVL says: all voltage must be used up — the resistor absorbs the leftover 1.3 V.
Step 2: Calculate Resistance
\(R = V / I = 1.3V / 0.02A = 65 \Omega\)
Step 3: Select Standard Value
Nearest standard: **68 . Always round UP for safety. 100 is also common for “dimmer but safer” LEDs.*










Circuit labels



Supply: 3.3V
\(V_R\) = 1.3V (resistor absorbs this)
\(V_{LED}\) = 2.0V
Current: 19 mA ✓ (safe!)

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
text:
Reading a Schematic: The Flow
To read a circuit like a pro, think of it as a Gravity System:


1. The Vertical Flow \(\downarrow\)

High Pressure (+V) is at the TOP.
The Drain (GND) is at the BOTTOM.
Current always flows “downhill” from Power to Ground.

2. The Horizontal Logic \(\rightarrow\)

Inputs (Sensors/Switches) on the Left.
Outputs (LEDs/Motors) on the Right.
Read connections like a book: Left to Right.









What you’re reading above


+5V (top) → Resistor (zigzag) → LED (arrow-triangle) → GND (bottom). Every complete schematic follows this downhill pattern.

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
text: Interactive: Voltage Loops

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
text:
KVL: Conservation of Energy


The Principle: Every volt added by the supply must be dropped (used up) somewhere in the loop. You cannot return to the battery with “leftover” pressure.
\[\text{Voltage}_{\text{Supply}} = V_1 + V_2 + V_3 \dots\]







The Roller Coaster Analogy


The chain lift pulls you up to the peak (+V). Every drop and loop after that uses up that height. You must be at 0 height to return to the start.

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
text:
Interactive: Current Branches


Observation: Watching the “moving charges” (dots). At the junction, the flow splits into two paths. Does any charge disappear at the junction? Is the total flow in equal to the total flow out?

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
text:
KCL: Conservation of Charge


The Principle: Whatever current flows into a junction (node) must flow out of it. Electricity isn’t a gas; it can’t “compress” or pile up.
\[\text{Current}_{\text{In}} = I_1 + I_2 + I_3 \dots\]







The Highway Analogy


Think of a highway interchange. 100 cars enter. If 40 exit West and 60 exit East, all 100 are accounted for. No cars vanish.

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
text:
KVL in Practice: The “Leftover” Voltage
Why do we need a resistor for a simple LED?



The Supply: Your battery provides 5V.
The LED: A standard Red LED only needs 2V.
The Problem: Without a resistor, the extra 3V of pressure will force too much current into the LED—making it go POP.
The Solution: A resistor “absorbs” that extra 3V, keeping the LED safe.


\[V_{supply} = V_{resistor} + V_{LED}\] \[5V = 3V + 2V \checkmark\]










Read the labels


The 3V drop happens across the resistor (zigzag). The 2V drop happens across the LED. They sum to exactly 5V. KVL holds.

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
text:
KCL in Practice: Parallel Paths
In robotics, we often have many components (motors, sensors, LEDs) sharing the same battery.

The Junction: The battery’s positive rail acts as a massive junction.
The Split: Current flows out of the battery and splits into each component path.
The Sum: Every milliamp drawn by a motor or LED is added together. This is why high-power robots need thick wires—to carry the sum of all those currents!

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
text:
From Schematic to Real Life
The schematic tells you the logic. The breadboard makes it physical.


Schematic (Logic)



Top wire = Power rail
Zigzag = Resistor
Arrow = LED



Breadboard (Physical)



Red rail = Power source
Row 5 = Connection point
Blue rail = Ground return

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
text:
Putting It Together
Let’s trace one complete circuit through all three lenses:



1. The Schematic
\[\text{+5V} \rightarrow \underbrace{330\Omega}_{\text{resistor}} \rightarrow \underbrace{\text{LED}}_{\text{2V drop}} \rightarrow \text{GND}\]


2. The Math (KVL + Ohm’s Law)
\(V_R = 5V - 2V = 3V\) \(I = 3V / 330\Omega = 9\text{ mA}\) ✓ (safe!)


3. The Build Plan

Wire 5V → Red Rail, GND → Blue Rail
Resistor: red rail → Row 5
LED: Row 5 (+) → Row 6 (−)
Row 6 → Blue Rail











Next Up: Part 9 — The Lab


You’ll build exactly this circuit — then extend it to 3 LEDs (traffic light!) and add a light sensor. The schematic → breadboard → code pipeline you just learned is the full workflow.

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
Kirchhoff’s Voltage Law (KVL)


Shared Energy
Fundamental Principle: Conservation of Energy
Every volt provided by the power source must be “dropped” (used up) by the components.






Key Concept: In a single loop (Series), the Current (\(I\)) is constant everywhere, but the Voltage (\(V\)) is shared among components.



\[V_{total} = V_1 + V_2 + \dots\]








The Loop Rule


Think of a waterfall. If you start at height \(H\), you must drop a total of \(H\) distance (via one or many falls) before reaching the bottom.

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
text:
Resistors in Series
When components are connected end-to-end, they form a Series circuit.


Characteristics:

Constant Current: The current \(I\) has only one path; it is the same through all components.
Voltage Division: The supply voltage splits across each resistor.
Total Resistance: \(R_{total}\) is the sum of all individual resistances.

\[R_{total} = R_1 + R_2 + R_3 \dots\]


Adding more resistors in series increases total resistance and decreases total current flow.

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
Kirchhoff’s Current Law (KCL)


Split Flow
Fundamental Principle: Conservation of Charge
Electric charge cannot be created or destroyed. Whatever current flows into a junction must flow out of it.






Key Concept: In a junction (Parallel), the Voltage (\(V\)) across branches is equal, but the Current (\(I\)) is split (\(I_{in} = I_1 + I_2\)).



\[I_{total} = I_1 + I_2 + \dots\]








The Junction Rule


Think of parallel pipes. If 1 liter per second enters a T-junction, exactly 1 liter per second must exit through the combined branches.

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
text:
Resistors in Parallel
When components are connected across the same two nodes, they form a Parallel circuit.


Characteristics:

Constant Voltage: Every branch sees the exact same voltage (potential difference).
Current Division: The total current \(I_{tot}\) splits based on the resistance of each branch.
Total Resistance: \(R_{eq}\) decreases as you add more paths.

\[\frac{1}{R_{total}} = \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2} + \frac{1}{R_3} \dots\]


Adding more resistors in parallel decreases total resistance and increases total current!

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
text:
Interactive: Pipe Logic (KCL)


Observation: Hover over the split point with your mouse to use the Probe. Notice that \(Q_{total} = Q_1 + Q_2\). Even if you restrict one branch, the total flow logic holds.

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
text:
Summary: Series vs. Parallel








Feature
Series
Parallel




Path
Single Path
Multiple Branches


Current (\(I\))
Same through all
Splits between branches


Voltage (\(V\))
Splits across resistors
Same across all branches


Resistance
Increases
Decreases









Tip


In parallel, the total resistance is ALWAYS smaller than the smallest individual resistor!

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
Schematic Layout Rules
Schematics aren’t random; they follow a strict “gravity” and “reading” direction to prevent head-aches.


1. Vertical Gravity \(\downarrow\)

High Potential (+V) is at the TOP.
The Reference (GND) is at the BOTTOM.
Electricity “falls” downhill.

2. Horizontal Reading \(\rightarrow\)

Inputs (Sensors) on the Left.
Outputs (LEDs) on the Right.








Professional Tip


Always draw your “GND” symbols pointing down. It makes the schematic instantly readable as a circuit.

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
text:
Engineering Math: The Safe LED
Why do we need a resistor?
KVL says: \(V_{supply} = V_{resistor} + V_{LED}\).
The LED will consume a 2V drop and the maximum current it can handle is 20 mA.



Find the “Leftover” Voltage
\(V_{resistor} = V_{supply} - V_{led}
              = 3.3V - 2.0V = \mathbf{1.3V}\)
Calculate Resistance (Ohm’s Law)
\(R = V / I = 1.3V / 0.02A = \mathbf{65 \Omega}\)
Select Standard Value
Nearest standard: 68 \(\Omega\). (Always round UP for safety).

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
text:
The “Finger Test”






Safety Tip


If you build a circuit and a component feels hot to the touch—UNPLUG IT.
Something is wired incorrectly, or a resistor is too small, allowing too much “friction” (heat).

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
Rule #1: The Safety Buffer






Never connect 5V to GND directly


Always use a “buffer” component (like a resistor or LED) to regulate the flow of energy.





The Physics (Ohm’s Law)
\(I = V / R = 5V / 0\Omega = \mathbf{\infty A}\)

The Real World

USB Safety Limit: ~500 mA
A “Short” pulls: Thousands of mA









Result


The “excess” energy has nowhere to go but into heat. This is why components melt or smell like burning.

title: Session 2: Make It Move & Remote Control
text:
The “Muscle” vs The “Brain”
We learned that the Arduino is the Brain (it sends 0s and 1s using low-voltage signals).
Motors are the Muscles. They require a large flow of electricity (current) to generate magnetic fields strong enough to spin wheels.

LED Requires: ~20 milliAmps (mA)
Arduino Pin Maximum: ~40 milliAmps (mA)
DC Motor Requires: ~500 to 1,000+ milliAmps (mA)

title: Session 2: Make It Move & Remote Control
text:
Rule #1 of Robotics






Never Power a Motor Directly From an Arduino Pin!


If you try to draw 1,000 mA from a pin designed to supply only 40 mA, you will instantly melt the internal circuitry of the Arduino chip.




Think of it like trying to fill a swimming pool using a drinking straw. The pressure will destroy the straw.

title: Session 2: Movement + Problem Patterns
text:
The Solution: The Motor Driver
To protect the Arduino, we use a component called a Motor Driver (like the L298N).


How it works:

Separate Power: The motor gets its heavy power directly from the battery pack.
Logic Control: The Arduino sends a tiny, safe HIGH or LOW signal to the Motor Driver.
The Switch: The Motor Driver opens a “heavy-duty valve” that lets the battery power flow to the motor.

title: Session 2: Make It Move
section: Why we simulate before we build
text:
Why we simulate before we build
Wiring a motor driver wrong can short-circuit the battery or blow up components. We will use Tinkercad to wire our first virtual motor driver. If we make a mistake, we only blow up pixels!






Your Task


Go to tinkercad.com/circuits and start a new project.

title: Session 2: Make It Move
text:
Wiring the L298N Motor Driver


We need to connect 3 types of wires:

Power In: Battery + to 12V, Battery - to GND.
Motor Out: Motor wires to OUT1 and OUT2.
Logic In: IN1 (Pin 9), IN2 (Pin 10), and ENA (Pin 11).

And crucially, Arduino GND must connect to the Battery GND so they share the same 0V reference!
















Wait, what is 12V vs 5V?


The driver takes the strong battery power (up to 12V) on one side, and the safe 5V logic signals from the Arduino on the other side.

Transition to Reality
Now it’s time to build the real thing.






Safety First

title: Session 2: Make It Move & Remote Control
text:
Step 1: Chassis Assembly
The chassis is the physical body of the robot.

Mount the DC motors to the frame using the provided brackets.
Attach the wheels to the motor shafts.
Secure the caster wheel (the tiny front wheel) to the front of the frame.
Mount the Arduino and the L298N Motor Driver to the top deck.

title: Session 2: Make It Move & Remote Control
text:
Step 2: Power Connections
We must route the power carefully.


From the Battery Pack:

Red wire (+) \(\rightarrow\) Motor Driver 12V
Black wire (-) \(\rightarrow\) Motor Driver GND

From the Arduino:

Arduino GND \(\rightarrow\) Motor Driver GND
Do not connect Arduino 5V to the motor power!





Power & Common Ground

title: Session 2: Make It Move & Remote Control
text:
Step 3: Wiring the Motors
Connect the physical motor wires.

Left motor red/black wires \(\rightarrow\) Motor Driver OUT1 & OUT2.
Right motor red/black wires \(\rightarrow\) Motor Driver OUT3 & OUT4.

(Don’t worry too much about matching red and black perfectly here; if a motor spins backward later, we can fix it by simply swapping the wires or changing our code!)

title: Session 2: Make It Move & Remote Control
text:
Step 4: The Logic Wires
Finally, connect the Arduino pins to the Motor Driver inputs. Use jumper wires:

Left Motor: Pin 4 \(\rightarrow\) Direction (ML_Ctrl), Pin 5 \(\rightarrow\) Speed (ML_PWM)
Right Motor: Pin 2 \(\rightarrow\) Direction (MR_Ctrl), Pin 6 \(\rightarrow\) Speed (MR_PWM)







Pin 3 is Reserved


Do not use Pin 3 for the motor driver — it is reserved for the IR receiver in the next part.









Speed Control


The PWM pins (ML_PWM, MR_PWM) control how fast each motor spins. They must be connected to PWM-capable pins (marked with a ~ on the Arduino), so we can change the speed in our code!

text: Motor Logic: Polarity & Direction

title: Session 2: Make It Move & Remote Control
text:
The Manual Problem






Can we swap wires while driving?


To make a robot reverse, we would have to stop it, unplug the wires, swap them, and plug them back in.



This is impossible for a computer to do by itself… unless we use electronic switches.

title: Session 2: Make It Move & Remote Control
text:
The Solution: The H-Bridge
An H-Bridge is a circuit of 4 electronic switches. By opening and closing different pairs, we can “redirect” the electricity flow without touching a single wire!



To go Forward: Close S1 and S4.
To go Backward: Close S2 and S3.
To Stop: Open all switches.

Caution: Never close S1 and S3 at the same time (Short Circuit!)

title: Session 2: Movement + Problem Patterns
text:
The Motor Driver (L298N)
You don’t need to build an H-Bridge from tiny switches. It’s already built into the Motor Driver chip!


How it works:

The Battery gives the heavy power (Muscle).
The Arduino sends 5V signals (Brain) to say “Close S1 and S4”.
The Chip does the heavy lifting, protecting the Arduino from the 1,000 mA current.

Invisible Control
How does a TV remote talk to the TV? It blinks a light that we can’t see!

Infrared (IR) is light with a frequency lower than red light.
Our eyes can’t see it, but the robot’s secondary “electronic eye” can.
It’s like a flashlight sending a secret Morse code at high speed.

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
text:
The Math: Solving Series Circuits
In a series circuit, components are connected end-to-end, forming a single path.

















The Assumptions



KCL: Current (\(I\)) is the same everywhere. \(I_{total} = I_1 = I_2 = \dots\)
KVL: Source voltage equals the sum of drops. \(V_{total} = V_1 + V_2 + \dots\)





The Derivation

Apply Ohm’s Law (\(V = IR\)) to each: \(V_1 = I \cdot R_1, \quad V_2 = I \cdot R_2 \dots\)
Substitute into KVL: \(V_{total} = (I \cdot R_1) + (I \cdot R_2) \dots\)
Use Equivalent Resistance (\(R_{eq}\)): \(I \cdot R_{eq} = I(R_1 + R_2 + \dots)\)
Divide by \(I\): \(\mathbf{R_{eq} = R_1 + R_2 + \dots + R_n}\)

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
text:
The Math: Solving Parallel Circuits
In a parallel circuit, resistors are connected across the same two nodes.

















The Assumptions



KVL: Voltage (\(V\)) is identical across all. \(V_{total} = V_1 = V_2 = \dots\)
KCL: Total current splits among branches. \(I_{total} = I_1 + I_2 + \dots\)





The Derivation

Apply Ohm’s Law (\(I = V/R\)): \(I_1 = V/R_1, \quad I_2 = V/R_2 \dots\)
Substitute into KCL: \(I_{total} = \frac{V}{R_1} + \frac{V}{R_2} \dots\)
Use Equivalent Resistance (\(R_{eq}\)): \(\frac{V}{R_{eq}} = V \left( \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2} \dots \right)\)
Divide by \(V\): \(\mathbf{\frac{1}{R_{eq}} = \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2} + \dots + \frac{1}{R_n}}\)

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
Summary Table









Connection
Constant Value
Physical Law Used
Resulting Formula




Series
Current (\(I\))
KVL (\(V_1 + V_2 + \dots = V_{total}\))
\(R_{eq} = R_1 + R_2 + \dots\)


Parallel
Voltage (\(V\))
KCL (\(I_{in} = I_1 + I_2 + \dots\))
\(\frac{1}{R_{eq}} = \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2} + \dots\)









Tip


In parallel, the total resistance is ALWAYS smaller than the smallest individual resistor!

The Real H-Bridge


Building an H-Bridge from tiny transistors is hard.
Instead, we use the L298N Module. It’s a “dual” driver that can control two motors.
It gives us 3 types of pins:

IN Pins: Controls Direction (Forward/Backward)
EN Pins: Controls Speed (The Gas Pedal!)





The L298N Motor Driver








Tinkercad Note


Tinkercad does not have the L298N “Red Board.” Instead, we will use the L293D chip, which is the H-Bridge “brain” inside these modules. The logic is exactly the same!

In a series circuit, components are connected end-to-end, forming a single path.

KVL: Total voltage equals the sum of drops. \(V_{total} = V_1 + V_2 + \dots\)

title: Session 2: Movement + Problem Patterns
text:
Wiring the L293D Driver (Chip)


We connect the L293D chip across the middle of a breadboard.

Power: Battery + to Pin 8, Arduino 5V to Pin 16, and GND to Pin 4.
Motor: Motor wires to Pin 3 and Pin 6.
Logic: IN1 (Pin 9) \(\rightarrow\) Pin 2, IN2 (Pin 10) \(\rightarrow\) Pin 7, and ENA (Pin 11) \(\rightarrow\) Pin 1.

Make sure Arduino GND and Battery GND are both connected to Pin 4!
















Pins 4, 5, 12, 13


On the L293D, all four of these pins are connected to GND and also help act as a “heat sink” to keep the chip cool.

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
text:
Case Study: The Robot’s Eyes
You want to add two Red LEDs for a robot’s eyes. You have a 5V battery. Should you wire them in Series or Parallel?


Option A: Series

Apply KVL: Each LED “costs” 2V. \(2V + 2V = \mathbf{4V}\) total drop.
The Result: 5V supply is enough!
Value: Battery lasts longer because current only flows through one path (KCL).


Option B: Parallel

Apply KCL: Each LED needs 20mA. Total flow = \(20mA + 20mA = \mathbf{40mA}\).
The Result: Battery drains twice as fast.
Value: If one LED breaks, the other stays on! (Independence).

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
section: Why do we care? The Predictor
text:
Why do we care? The Predictor
Without these laws, engineering is just guessing. With them, you are a Predictor.

KVL predicts if you have enough “pressure” to turn things on.
KCL predicts how long your battery will last.
Ohm’s Law predicts if your component will survive or explode.

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
text:
Step-by-Step: The Safe LED
Why do we need a resistor?
KVL says: \(V_{supply} = V_{resistor} + V_{LED}\).
The LED will consume a 2V drop and the maximum current it can handle is 20 mA.


The Mission: Don’t Fried the LED
Calculate the resistor needed for a Red LED (2V drop, 20mA target) on a 5V Arduino pin.

Identify the Pressure (Voltage) \(V_{supply} = 5V\)
Apply KVL (Find the “Leftover”) Resistor must “soak up” what the LED doesn’t. \(V_{resistor} = 5V - 2.0V = \mathbf{3.0V}\)
Apply Ohm’s Law (Find the Width) \(R = V / I = 3.0V / 0.02A = \mathbf{150 \Omega}\)
The Engineering Choice Standard resistors: \(150 \Omega, 220 \Omega, 330 \Omega\). Pick 220 \(\Omega\) to be safe. (Lower current = longer life).

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
text:
The Detective: Troubleshooting with Laws
When a circuit doesn’t work, don’t just “wiggle the wires.” Use the laws to find the culprit:


The Case of the Dead LED
Clue: You connected everything, but there is no light.

The KVL Scan: Measure voltage across the Battery.

Result: 5V. (Energy is entrying).

The KVL Scan: Measure voltage across the LED.

Result: 0V. (Wait! Where did the pressure go?)

The Conclusion: If \(V_{led} = 0\), then according to KVL, the “pressure” is hanging out somewhere else—likely a broken wire or a bad connection that has “Infinite Resistance.”








Scientific Luck


Troubleshooting without laws is luck. Troubleshooting with laws is science.

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
Case Study 1: The Robot’s Eyes
The Problem: You want to add two Red LEDs as a robot’s eyes.


Given Information



Parameter
Value




Supply Voltage
5V battery


LED Forward Voltage
2V each


LED Target Current
20 mA each



Your decision: Wire them in Series or Parallel?







The Goal


For each wiring option, we need to:

Find the correct resistor to protect each LED.
Analyse the voltage across each component and the total current drawn.

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
text:
Case Study 1 — Option A: Series



















Step-by-Step Calculation

Apply KVL — Check voltage drops sum to supply
\[V_{supply} = V_R + V_{Eye1} + V_{Eye2}\] \[5V = V_R + 2V + 2V \implies V_R = \mathbf{1V}\]
Find the single current (same everywhere in series)
\[I = \frac{V_R}{R} = \frac{1V}{150\,\Omega} = \mathbf{6.7\,mA}\]
Voltage at each point in the loop

After resistor: \(5V - 1V = \mathbf{4V}\) remaining
After Eye 1: \(4V - 2V = \mathbf{2V}\) remaining
After Eye 2: \(2V - 2V = \mathbf{0V}\) (back to GND ✓)

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
text:
Case Study 1 — Option B: Parallel



















Step-by-Step Calculation

Apply KVL — Voltage across each branch
Both branches are connected to the same 5V: \[V_R = V_{supply} - V_{LED} = 5V - 2V = \mathbf{3V}\]
Current in each branch (Ohm’s Law)
\[I_{Eye1} = \frac{3V}{150\,\Omega} = \mathbf{20\,mA}\] \[I_{Eye2} = \frac{3V}{150\,\Omega} = \mathbf{20\,mA}\]
Total current drawn (KCL at junction)
\[I_{total} = I_{Eye1} + I_{Eye2} = 20 + 20 = \mathbf{40\,mA}\]

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
Case Study 1 — The Verdict









Series
Parallel




Voltage per LED
2V each (shared from 5V)
2V each (full 5V available)


Current per LED
~6.7 mA (dimmer)
20 mA (full brightness)


Total Current
~6.7 mA
40 mA


Battery Life
✅ Longer
❌ Drains 6× faster


If one LED breaks
❌ Both go dark
✅ Other stays on









Pick Series for battery life. Pick Parallel for reliability and full brightness.

section: Case Study 2: The Safe LED — Setup
text:
Case Study 2: The Safe LED — Setup
The Problem: An Arduino pin outputs 5V. A Red LED needs 2V and max 20 mA. What resistor protects it?






KVL says: \(V_{supply} = V_{resistor} + V_{LED}\)
The resistor must “absorb” whatever voltage the LED doesn’t use.















What We Know



Quantity
Value




\(V_{supply}\)
5V


\(V_{LED}\)
2V


\(I_{target}\)
20 mA


\(R = ?\)
❓

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
text:
Case Study 2: Calculating the Resistor

Step 1 — Apply KVL (voltage across the resistor)
\[V_{R} = V_{supply} - V_{LED} = 5V - 2V = \mathbf{3V}\]
Step 2 — Apply Ohm’s Law (find resistance)
\[R = \frac{V_R}{I} = \frac{3V}{0.02\,A} = \mathbf{150\,\Omega}\]
Step 3 — Verify current through the circuit
\[I = \frac{V_R}{R} = \frac{3V}{150\,\Omega} = \mathbf{20\,mA} \checkmark\]
Step 4 — The Engineering Choice
Standard resistors: \(150\,\Omega\), \(220\,\Omega\), \(330\,\Omega\)
→ Pick \(220\,\Omega\) to be safe (slightly lower current → longer LED life)
\[I_{actual} = \frac{3V}{220\,\Omega} \approx \mathbf{13.6\,mA} \quad \text{(safe ✓)}\]

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
Case Study 1 — Option A: Series (Circuit)












The Topology

KVL: Total voltage supplied (5V) must be shared.
KCL: Only one path exists; current (\(I\)) is constant.
Goal: First find the resistor \(R\), then analyse the circuit.

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
text:
Case Study 1 — Option A: Series (Calculation)
Step-by-Step Calculation

Apply KVL — How much voltage is left for the resistor?
\[V_R = V_{supply} - V_{Eye1} - V_{Eye2} = 5V - 2V - 2V = \mathbf{1V}\]
Apply Ohm’s Law — Find the required resistor
We want \(I = 20\,\text{mA}\) (the LED’s rated current) flowing through the loop: \[R = \frac{V_R}{I} = \frac{1V}{0.02\,A} = \mathbf{50\,\Omega} \rightarrow \text{pick standard } \mathbf{68\,\Omega}\]
Verify — Actual current with chosen resistor
\[I = \frac{V_R}{R} = \frac{1V}{68\,\Omega} \approx \mathbf{14.7\,mA} \quad \text{(safe ✓)}\]
Voltage at each point in the loop

After resistor: \(5V - 1V = \mathbf{4V}\) remaining
After Eye 1: \(4V - 2V = \mathbf{2V}\) remaining
After Eye 2: \(2V - 2V = \mathbf{0V}\) (back to GND ✓)

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
Case Study 1 — Option B: Parallel (Circuit)












The Topology

KVL: Each branch gets full pressure (5V).
KCL: Total current splits among branches.
Goal: First find the resistor \(R\) for each branch, then sum the currents.

title: Session 1: Circuits Basics
text:
Case Study 1 — Option B: Parallel (Calculation)
Step-by-Step Calculation

Apply KVL — How much voltage falls across each resistor?
Each branch sees the full 5V, and the LED drops 2V: \[V_R = V_{supply} - V_{LED} = 5V - 2V = \mathbf{3V}\]
Apply Ohm’s Law — Find the required resistor per branch
We want \(I = 20\,\text{mA}\) through each LED: \[R = \frac{V_R}{I} = \frac{3V}{0.02\,A} = \mathbf{150\,\Omega} \rightarrow \text{pick standard } \mathbf{150\,\Omega}\]
Verify branch current with chosen resistor
\[I_{Eye1} = \frac{3V}{150\,\Omega} = \mathbf{20\,mA}, \quad I_{Eye2} = \frac{3V}{150\,\Omega} = \mathbf{20\,mA} \quad \text{(safe ✓)}\]
Total current drawn (KCL at junction)
\[I_{total} = I_{Eye1} + I_{Eye2} = 20 + 20 = \mathbf{40\,mA}\]

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
section: The Detective: The Case of the Dead LED
text:
The Detective: The Case of the Dead LED
When a circuit doesn’t work, don’t just “wiggle the wires.” Use the laws to find the invisible culprit.
You’ve connected everything perfectly—or so you think—but the LED remains dark.

The KVL Scan (Source): Measure voltage at the battery.

Result: 5V. (The energy is there).

The KVL Scan (Load): Measure voltage across the LED.

Result: 0V. (Wait… where did the pressure go?)

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
The Detective’s Verdict


The Solution
If \(V_{led} = 0V\) but the battery has \(5V\), according to KVL, that 5V must be dropped somewhere else.

The Culprit: Infinite Resistance
A broken wire, a loose connection, or a component not touching the breadboard strips creates a “gap” that steals all the pressure.



“Troubleshooting without laws is luck.
Troubleshooting with laws is science.”









Scientific Method



Observe: LED is OFF.
Measure: \(V_{battery} = 5V, V_{LED} = 0V\).
Deduce: The “missing” 5V is trapped at a bad connection.

The Three Things Every Robot Does

Ultrasonic sensor, IR receiver, light sensor — the robot feels the world.

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
But First — Why Electricity?

Everything in your robot runs on electricity.


To build a robot, you need to understand one big idea:

Electricity is like water flowing through pipes.



Let’s see how.

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
The Two Safety Rules
Before we touch anything, two rules that will save your robot every time:

🛡️ Always use a resistor with an LED — without one, too much current flows and the LED burns out
⚡ Never connect 5V to GND with a bare wire — it’s called a short circuit and it can fry your Arduino








The Finger Test


If a component feels hot to the touch — unplug immediately. Too much current is flowing somewhere.

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
The Narrow Pipe Rule



A narrow pipe slows down water flow
A resistor does the same thing for electricity
The narrower (higher resistance) → the less current flows
This protects LEDs from getting too much








In the Kit


Your Keyestudio kit comes with pre-selected resistors for each LED. You don’t need to calculate — just use the right colour band!
For the traffic light: 330 Ω (orange-orange-brown) ✓

Find
Formula
Units




Voltage
V = I × R

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
🔥 Bonus — Exercise: Calculate Current
(recall) A battery provides 5 V. A resistor of 100 Ω is connected. How much current flows?
Show your calculation.

Answer: I = V / R = 5 / 100 = 0.05 A (50 mA)

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
🔥 Bonus — Exercise: Find the Resistor
(apply) An LED needs 2 V and 20 mA. Your Arduino gives 5 V. What resistor do you need?

Answer: V_resistor = 5 − 2 = 3 V · R = 3 / 0.02 = 150 Ω → pick 150 Ω or 220 Ω ✓

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
Variable Resistance: The Volume Knob

Think of a volume knob on a speaker — turn it to control how loud it gets
In electronics, this is called a potentiometer — it adjusts resistance smoothly
More resistance → less current → dimmer LED / slower motor

You can find this in: Volume knobs · Brightness dimmers · Fan speed controls



Position
What happens




Fully turned one way
Maximum resistance → minimum current


Middle
Medium resistance → medium current


Fully the other way
Zero resistance → maximum current

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
From Analog to Digital


Potentiometer (Analog)

Smooth range of values
Like a dimmer switch — infinite steps between off and fully on


A GPIO Pin (Digital)

Only two states: HIGH or LOW, ON or OFF
Like a light switch — no in-between




Your robot uses both: analog sensors (like the LDR) that give smooth values, and digital pins that decide HIGH or LOW.

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
section: 🔥 Bonus — The Automatic Valve: The Transistor
text:
🔥 Bonus — The Automatic Valve: The Transistor

For curious students: what if the valve could open itself?



Remember the valve that controls water flow?
A transistor is like a magic valve — instead of turning it with your hand, you open it with a tiny electrical signal.

🤏 Tiny signal in → 💧 Big current out
The bigger the signal, the more it opens
No signal at all → it stays completely closed














💡 Your Arduino sends a tiny signal — the transistor uses it to switch a motor or LED that needs far more power than the Arduino can provide directly.

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
🔥 Bonus — Transistors Make Logic

Transistor fully ON → effectively zero resistance → current flows → “1”
Transistor fully OFF → enormous resistance → no current → “0”

A billion of these = a CPU.

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
🔥 Bonus — Exercise: Transistor Gain
(recall) A transistor has a gain of 200. You apply 0.5 mA to the Base. How much current flows through the Collector?

Answer: I_C = Gain × I_B = 200 × 0.5 mA = 100 mA
A tiny 0.5 mA signal controls a 100 mA load — that is amplification.

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
🔥 Bonus — Exercise: Controlling a Motor
(apply) You want to control a 500 mA motor using an Arduino GPIO pin (max 40 mA). Is this possible? What is the transistor’s role?

Answer: Yes — but not directly. The GPIO cannot source 500 mA safely. A transistor with sufficient gain, driven by the GPIO, switches the 500 mA motor current from a separate supply. The GPIO controls the gate; the transistor carries the load. (This is exactly what the L298N motor driver in your kit does.)

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
section: 🔥 Bonus — Two Worlds: Analog vs. Digital
text:
🔥 Bonus — Two Worlds: Analog vs. Digital


Analog — Infinite Steps

Dimmer switch: 0% to 100% brightness
LDR sensor: variable (1 kΩ to 100 kΩ)


Digital — Two States Only

A light switch: ON or OFF
A GPIO pin: HIGH (5 V) or LOW (0 V)




The bridge: A transistor collapses an analog input voltage into a digital decision — above a threshold → 1, below it → 0. A billion of these decisions per second = a CPU.

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
Two Rules Before You Wire
You don’t need to memorise all of circuit design. Just remember two rules before every build:

🛡️ Always use a resistor with an LED — it limits the current so the LED doesn’t burn out
⚡ Never connect 5V directly to GND with a bare wire — this is a short circuit and it will damage your Arduino








How to wire the breadboard



Red rail (+) → Arduino 5V pin
Blue rail (−) → Arduino GND pin
All components plug into the rows in between

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
🔥 Bonus — Common Code Mistakes

For students who want to go deeper into C++ syntax.

C++ is very picky. Watch out for these:

The Missing Semicolon (;) — Every instruction must end with one. It’s like a period at the end of a sentence.
Case Sensitivity — digitalWrite is correct. digitalwrite or DigitalWrite will cause an error.
The Curly Braces ({ }) — They group code together. Every { needs a matching }.
Incorrect Pin Numbers — Make sure the number in your code matches the physical hole on the Arduino.







Error messages are your friends


If your code won’t upload, read the red text at the bottom. It usually tells you exactly which line has the problem.

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
🔥 Bonus — The Math: Series Resistance
In a series circuit, components are connected end-to-end, forming a single path.

















The Assumptions



KCL: Current (\(I\)) is the same everywhere. \(I_{total} = I_1 = I_2 = \dots\)
KVL: Total voltage equals the sum of drops. \(V_{total} = V_1 + V_2 + \dots\)





The Derivation

Apply Ohm’s Law (\(V = IR\)) to each: \(V_1 = I \cdot R_1, \quad V_2 = I \cdot R_2 \dots\)
Substitute into KVL: \(V_{total} = (I \cdot R_1) + (I \cdot R_2) \dots\)
Use Equivalent Resistance (\(R_{eq}\)): \(I \cdot R_{eq} = I(R_1 + R_2 + \dots)\)
Divide by \(I\): \(\mathbf{R_{eq} = R_1 + R_2 + \dots + R_n}\)

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
🔥 Bonus — The Math: Parallel Resistance
In a parallel circuit, resistors are connected across the same two nodes.

















The Assumptions



KVL: Voltage (\(V\)) is identical across all. \(V_{total} = V_1 = V_2 = \dots\)
KCL: Total current splits among branches. \(I_{total} = I_1 + I_2 + \dots\)





The Derivation

Apply Ohm’s Law (\(I = V/R\)): \(I_1 = V/R_1, \quad I_2 = V/R_2 \dots\)
Substitute into KCL: \(I_{total} = \frac{V}{R_1} + \frac{V}{R_2} \dots\)
Use Equivalent Resistance (\(R_{eq}\)): \(\frac{V}{R_{eq}} = V \left( \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2} \dots \right)\)
Divide by \(V\): \(\mathbf{\frac{1}{R_{eq}} = \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2} + \dots + \frac{1}{R_n}}\)

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
🔥 Bonus — Case Study 1: Series Calculation
Step-by-Step Calculation

Apply KVL — How much voltage is left for the resistor?
\[V_R = V_{supply} - V_{Eye1} - V_{Eye2} = 5V - 2V - 2V = \mathbf{1V}\]
Apply Ohm’s Law — Find the required resistor
We want \(I = 20\,\text{mA}\) (the LED’s rated current) flowing through the loop: \[R = \frac{V_R}{I} = \frac{1V}{0.02\,A} = \mathbf{50\,\Omega} \rightarrow \text{pick standard } \mathbf{68\,\Omega}\]
Verify — Actual current with chosen resistor
\[I = \frac{V_R}{R} = \frac{1V}{68\,\Omega} \approx \mathbf{14.7\,mA} \quad \text{(safe ✓)}\]
Voltage at each point in the loop

After resistor: \(5V - 1V = \mathbf{4V}\) remaining
After Eye 1: \(4V - 2V = \mathbf{2V}\) remaining
After Eye 2: \(2V - 2V = \mathbf{0V}\) (back to GND ✓)

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
text:
🔥 Bonus — Case Study 1: Parallel Calculation
Step-by-Step Calculation

Apply KVL — How much voltage falls across each resistor?
Each branch sees the full 5V, and the LED drops 2V: \[V_R = V_{supply} - V_{LED} = 5V - 2V = \mathbf{3V}\]
Apply Ohm’s Law — Find the required resistor per branch
We want \(I = 20\,\text{mA}\) through each LED: \[R = \frac{V_R}{I} = \frac{3V}{0.02\,A} = \mathbf{150\,\Omega} \rightarrow \text{pick standard } \mathbf{150\,\Omega}\]
Verify branch current with chosen resistor
\[I_{Eye1} = \frac{3V}{150\,\Omega} = \mathbf{20\,mA}, \quad I_{Eye2} = \frac{3V}{150\,\Omega} = \mathbf{20\,mA} \quad \text{(safe ✓)}\]
Total current drawn (KCL at junction)
\[I_{total} = I_{Eye1} + I_{Eye2} = 20 + 20 = \mathbf{40\,mA}\]

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
section: 🔥 Bonus — Case Study 2: Calculating the Resistor
text:
🔥 Bonus — Case Study 2: Calculating the Resistor

Step 1 — Apply KVL (voltage across the resistor)
\[V_{R} = V_{supply} - V_{LED} = 5V - 2V = \mathbf{3V}\]
Step 2 — Apply Ohm’s Law (find resistance)
\[R = \frac{V_R}{I} = \frac{3V}{0.02\,A} = \mathbf{150\,\Omega}\]
Step 3 — Verify current through the circuit
\[I = \frac{V_R}{R} = \frac{3V}{150\,\Omega} = \mathbf{20\,mA} \checkmark\]
Step 4 — The Engineering Choice
Standard resistors: \(150\,\Omega\), \(220\,\Omega\), \(330\,\Omega\)
→ Pick \(220\,\Omega\) to be safe (slightly lower current → longer LED life)
\[I_{actual} = \frac{3V}{220\,\Omega} \approx \mathbf{13.6\,mA} \quad \text{(safe ✓)}\]

Something is wired incorrectly, or a resistor is too small, allowing too much “friction” (heat).

title: Session 1: Electricity is Magic
section: 🔥 Bonus — Transistors = The Brain of Everything
text:
🔥 Bonus — Transistors = The Brain of Everything


Fully OPEN → current flows freely
→ we call this “1” (ON)
Fully CLOSED → no current at all
→ we call this “0” (OFF)




Signal
Valve
Meaning




Strong
Open
1


None
Closed
0






🤯 A modern computer chip has billions of tiny transistors — each one switching between 0 and 1 billions of times per second. That is how your phone runs games, videos, and apps — all from billions of tiny automatic valves!

title: Session 2: Make It Move

section: What can a moving robot actually do?
text:
What can a moving robot actually do?
A remote-controlled car is incredibly fun — but useful robots solve real problems on their own.

💊 Deliver medicine inside a hospital
🔍 Inspect dangerous pipes underground
📦 Move boxes in a warehouse
🚜 Navigate a farm autonomously


To do this safely, they need more than a human holding a remote control!

title: Session 2: Make It Move & Remote Control
text:
The Pattern Behind Every Robot
Every robot program follows the same 3-step loop:


1️⃣ Sense
Read the world — buttons, sensors, signals.

2️⃣ Decide
Run logic — if...else, functions.

3️⃣ Act
Drive motors, blink LEDs, make sounds.


Today you completed Act (Motors) and basic Sense (IR Receiver).

title: Session 3: IR Remote Control
text:
Invisible Control
How does a TV remote talk to the TV? It blinks a light that we can’t see!

Infrared (IR) is light with a frequency lower than red light.
Our eyes can’t see it, but the robot’s secondary “electronic eye” can.
It’s like a flashlight sending a secret Morse code at high speed.

title: Session 3: IR Remote Control

What are those codes?
When you press a button, the remote sends a number like 0xFF18E7.

This is a HEXADECIMAL number (Base-16).
It’s like a unique ID for that specific button.
If you use a different remote, the numbers will be different!

text:
Brainstorming: The “Stickiness” Problem






Problem


If you press “Forward” once, the robot starts moving. But if you let go, it keeps moving! Why?




The IR remote only sends a signal when it’s pressed.
The Robot doesn’t know you let go unless you tell it.
Solution Ideas:

Add a dedicated “Stop” button.
Use a timer (if no signal for 0.5s, stop).
(Advanced) Check for the “Repeat” code (0xFFFFFFFF).

Use delay() to control how long each movement lasts during the dance.
When you press OK, the car temporarily ignores other buttons and performs the sequence.

void victoryDance() {
  car_right(); delay(300);  // Quick spin right
  car_back();  delay(500);  // Back it up
  car_left();  delay(300);  // Quick spin left
  car_Stop();
}

Instead of an infrared remote, we are going to use Bluetooth! This means we can control our robot using a smartphone app, just like a real RC car or a drone.

title: Session 2: Make It Move & Remote Control
text:
The HC-05 Module
The HC-05 has four main pins we need to use:

VCC: Power (5V from Arduino)
GND: Ground
TXD: Transmit (Sends data to the Arduino)
RXD: Receive (Receives data from the Arduino)

VCC ➔ Arduino 5V

RXD ➔ Arduino TX (Pin 1)

title: Session 3: Building the Smart Radar
text:
What is a Servo Motor?
Unlike the DC motors that spin continuously round and round, a Servo Motor can be told to point at a specific angle (usually 0° to 180°).

Use cases: Steering wheels, robotic arms, radar sweeps.
Inside: A tiny DC motor, gears, and a potentiometer to know its exact position.

title: Session 3: Building the Smart Radar
text:
Wiring the Servo 🔌
The SG90 micro-servo has 3 wires:

Brown / Black Line: Ground (GND)
Red Line: Power (5V)
Orange / Yellow Line: Signal (Connect to a PWM pin like ~9)







Warning


Always double-check the wire colors! Plugging power into the signal pin might damage the servo.

title: Session 3: Building the Smart Radar

text:
How does it work? 🦇
The Ultrasonic Sensor (HC-SR04) works like a bat’s echolocation.

It sends out a high-frequency sound wave (Trigger).
The sound wave travels until it bounces off an object.
The sensor listens for the returning echo (Echo).

By measuring how long the sound takes to return, we calculate the exact distance!

title: Session 3: Building the Smart Radar
text:
Wiring the Sensor 🔌
The sensor has 4 pins:

VCC: 5V Power
GND: Ground
Trig: Connect to digital pin (e.g., 10) - Sends the sound!
Echo: Connect to digital pin (e.g., 11) - Hears the sound!

The 3-LED Distance Indicator 🚦
Goal: Create a visual distance indicator using 3 LEDs (e.g., Green, Yellow, Red).

Green LED: Turns on if the object is far (> 20 cm)
Yellow LED: Turns on if the object is medium distance (between 10 cm and 20 cm)
Red LED: Turns on if the object is very close (< 10 cm)

Activity: Wire 3 LEDs to different pins and write the if / else if / else logic!

title: Session 3: Building the Smart Radar
text:
What is a Potentiometer? 🎛️
Before we make things move, we need to understand how machines “know” where they are.
A Potentiometer (or “Pot”) is a dial or knob with a resistor inside. As you turn it, it changes the electrical resistance.

The Encoder Idea: By reading the voltage from the middle pin, Arduino knows exactly what angle the dial is at!
Devices use this simple trick to encode physical position into data.

title: Session 3: Building the Smart Radar
text:
Wiring a Potentiometer
It has 3 pins:

VCC (5V) - One side
GND - The other side
Signal (Analog Out) - The middle pin. (Connect to A0)

text:
The Distance Formula 🧮
Why is the calculation duration * 0.034 / 2 ?

Speed of Sound: Sound travels at about 343 meters per second (or 0.034 centimeters per microsecond).
Round Trip: The sound pulse travels to the object AND bounces back. So, we must divide the total time by 2 to find just the distance to the object!

title: Session 2: Make It Move & Remote Control
text:
🔍 Under the Hood: Transistors & Diodes
Building an H-Bridge from simple mechanical switches is helpful for understanding the logic, but real microchips use solid-state electronics.
Inside the L298N (and similar drivers), those simple “switches” are actually MOSFET Transistors. The circuit also includes Flyback Diodes to protect the Arduino and the chip from high-voltage electrical spikes generated when the motor stops.

title: Session 2: Make It Move & Remote Control
text:
The “Stickiness” Problem Solved!






Why the timer?


If you press “Forward” once, the robot starts moving. But if you let go, it keeps moving! The IR remote only sends a signal when it’s pressed. The Robot doesn’t know you let go.




We use a timer (lastSignalTime) to track the last received signal.
The remote sends a “Repeat” code every ~110ms when held down.
If more than 200ms passes without a signal, the robot automatically stops!

text:
Why Do We Need Auto-Stop?


The Problem 🏎️💨
If you press a button and let go, the car keeps moving forever!

The remote only sends signals while pressed.
It never sends a “stop” signal when released.


The Solution ⏱️
Use Arduino’s built-in stopwatch: millis().

Save Time: Record the time of every IR signal.
Check Time: If >200ms passes with no new signal, tell the car to stop.
